sutta: the buddha said monks a man is endowed with 8 qualities among who is endowed with 8 qualities is worthy of offerings worthy of gifts worthy of oblations deserves to be reverently saluted the world's peerless field for merit what a hearing among is virtuous he is learned number 2 number 3 he dwells strenuous steadfast energetic shirking not the burden of righteousness or wholesomeness number 4 he is a forest dweller having his bed and seed apart from mankind number 5 enduring likes and dislikes dwelling in continuous mastery of dislikes which arise number 6 enduring fear and dismay dwelling in continuous mastery of fear and dismay which arise number 7 he attains to the 4 jana states number 8 he destroys the asavas he who is endowed with these 8 qualities is worthy of offerings worthy of gifts worthy of oblations deserves to be reverently saluted the world's fearless field for merit that's the end of the sutta
commentary:
so these are the 8 qualities which makes the monk very virtuous and worthy of uh salutations number 1 is virtuous number 2 his learner that means he's learned in the dhamma the buddha's teachings uh more specifically the dhamma vinaya the sutas and the vinaya and then number 3 he is energetic always getting rid of unwholesome states of mind and developing wholesome states of mind number 4 he is a forest dweller that means he dwells in seclusion having his lodgings away from mankind number 5 is always [Music] trying to master dislikes which arise and number 6 is always trying to master fear which arise fear which arise is more likely in lonely places if among dwells alone in lonely places and sometimes at night [Music] there might be certain things certain sounds which make him afraid so he has to master the fear number 7 he attains to the 4 janas the 4 janas the jhanas are considered uttari manosa dhamma higher than human attainment that is why the person can attain the janus he is worthy of um reverence and the last 1 he destroys the asaba becomes liberated
